summary: DHH draws a parallel between physical exercise and creative work, arguing that both reliably lift the human spirit but become hardest to start precisely when you need them most. He describes the gap between knowing creation is beneficial and actually doing it. He warns against 'simulacra of creation' — environments that mimic productive work without producing anything meaningful. The post concludes with an encouragement to push through inertia and start making things, because momentum builds on itself.
key_points: Creation and exercise share the same paradox: they're hardest to start when you need them most
Performative busyness — work that mimics creation without producing results — is especially corrosive to motivation
Negative streaks of inaction are self-reinforcing, making each day harder to break free from
The energy for creative work comes from doing it, not from waiting until you feel ready
money_quotes: It's hard not to see your spirits lifted when you're part of bringing something to life.
The hard part is getting started when you need it the most!
That's the curious chasm between knowledge and action.
Those periods were especially depressing when they felt like a simulacra of creation.
The pretending of purpose. The appearance of collaboration. Without any thrust towards completion.
For every day you stay there, the harder it is to get up, so best to muster everything you got to escape the dulling inertia now.
Once you're back to creating, the energy comes by itself.
key_insight: Creative work is its own cure for malaise, but performative busyness masquerading as creation is a trap that deepens the inertia it pretends to address.
